title: AWS Community Day (30/05/2026)
content:
Community Day AWS Report — 30/05/2026 Event purpose Share AWS, AI, and soft-skills knowledge within the First Cloud Journey / FCAJ community Showcase hackathon projects and challenger products Connect learners at the AWS Vietnam office Event details Event name: AWS Community Day (Event CD AWS) — First Cloud Journey AI Date: May 30, 2026 Location: 26th Floor — AWS Vietnam Office (Bitexco Tower, 2 Hai Trieu Street, Saigon Ward, Ho Chi Minh City) Role: Attendee Event photos: Google Drive folder Speakers / sessions 1. Huỳnh Thái Linh — Cloud Quest & Floci Level Up Your AWS Skills with Cloud Quest and Floci — learning AWS via Cloud Quest, billing awareness, and local simulation tools (Floci vs LocalStack).
2. Team The Ballers (FCAJ Challenger) — Hackathon & SynthHunter Huỳnh Anh Khương, Mai Quốc Anh, Nguyễn Trần Minh Quân shared their hackathon journey and demoed SynthHunter — an AI voice verification system that classifies speech as HUMAN / AI GENERATED / NEEDS REVIEW (XLS-R, Whisper, Fourier).
3. Nguyễn Thị Quỳnh Như — Why We Always Need Confidence Soft skills: why confidence is a must-have for career and campus life; agenda covering self-doubt, the science behind the struggle, and everyday confidence “hacks”.
4. Nghĩa Trần — Tử vi Đại Việt Introduced the Tử vi Đại Việt product/idea (destiny insights / self-understanding), plus GenAI context and product vision/differentiation.
5. Trần Minh Quân — The Hidden Iceberg of a Project: DevOps Before Disaster A talk on the “hidden iceberg” of a project: adopting a DevOps mindset before disaster — surfacing delivery, operations, and collaboration risks early instead of reacting only after failure.
6. Khac Uy Pham (HGU) — Procrastination A Familiar Enemy / The Iceberg of Procrastination — reframing delay as fear (not laziness), with the closing message “GO BUILD — Starting today”.
Group photo Takeaways & application AWS learning path: Cloud Quest + billing awareness when practicing on a real account (relevant to WebFood deploy). Hackathon & AI project: packaging a clear problem–solution (SynthHunter) and teamwork under time pressure. Soft skills: confidence and anti-procrastination habits for long internship projects. Product / DevOps thinking: surface the project’s “hidden iceberg” early (DevOps before disaster), instead of waiting until failure. Experience Community Day mixed AWS learning, hackathon/AI demos, community products, and soft skills. The AWS Bitexco venue plus First Cloud Journey branding made the community atmosphere tangible.
Overall, Event 1 broadened my view beyond the serverless lab — from AWS learning and AI projects to product thinking and soft skills.

title: Blog 1 — AI Agents on Amazon Bedrock
content:
AI Agents on Amazon Bedrock & AgentCore — AI that does work, not just answers Hi everyone,
While learning about AWS and Generative AI, I often read AWS Blog posts to understand how companies apply technology in practice. Recently I studied how AWS builds AI Agent systems with Amazon Bedrock and AgentCore, and found this approach especially interesting.
At first I expected these articles to focus on AI models or LLM capability. After digging deeper, the most valuable insight was not the model itself, but how AWS designs a system that lets AI actually perform work.
An AI Agent is not just a chatbot Previously, when I thought about AI, I usually imagined chatbots or Q&A systems.
With AI Agent architecture on AWS, AI goes beyond answering questions. It can:
Understand goals Plan Execute steps Observe results Adjust actions continuously This makes AI behave more like a system operator than a simple assistant tool.
System architecture: AI does not do everything alone What I found most interesting is how AWS designs the architecture.
AI does not handle the entire system directly. It sits inside a clear flow:
The user sends a request through CloudFront + API Gateway Lambda handles intermediate logic The request enters the AgentCore Runtime An Orchestrator Agent coordinates sub-agents Those agents use Tools (APIs / services) to take action The key idea: AI does not replace the system — it works with the system through APIs.
Multi-Agent: Split work like a real team Another strong point is that the system does not rely on a single AI. It uses multiple agents:
A Find agent for information gathering A Create agent for planning An Execute agent for taking action This mirrors a real team: analyst, designer, and implementer. As a result, the system is easier to scale, debug, and reason about.
Tool Use — the most important key My main takeaway: AI becomes truly useful only when it can use tools.
In this architecture:
AI does not operate the system directly Every action goes through APIs, databases, or external services This makes the system more stable, easier to swap AI models, and less dependent on one technology.
Applications go beyond AI or DevOps Once I understood the model, I realized it can apply to many use cases:
Automated website / app testing (like QA) Automated support ticket handling DevOps automation (deploy, monitor) Smart data pipelines Business AI assistants By changing only the tools an agent is allowed to use, the same system can be reused.
Personal perspective The most important lesson for me: the value of AI is not “smart answers,” but getting real work done.
In this system, Amazon Bedrock is not only a place to run models. It is a platform for building AI Agents, connecting them to systems, and automating workflows.
This completely changed how I see AI: not just a tool → but a component inside the system.
Conclusion For me, this is not merely an AI architecture. It is a new approach to building systems:
AI does not stand alone AI does not replace the system AI becomes a worker inside the system If you are learning about Amazon Bedrock, AI Agents, or Generative AI on AWS, this is a direction worth studying — because it shows how the technology is applied in practice, not only in demos.
Facebook post link:
https://www.facebook.com/groups/awsstudygroupfcj/permalink/2210158863082407/

title: DynamoDB
content:
DynamoDB Console: DynamoDB → Create table webfood-connections-prod
Partition key: connectionId (String) GSI: byUserId with partition key userId Capacity: On-demand; enable TTL attribute ttl Next: 5.4.2 SQS.

title: EventBridge Rules
content:
EventBridge Rules Console: EventBridge → Rules → select bus webfood-events-prod → Create rule (twice).
Rule 1 — webfood-to-queue-prod → SQS Worker Field Value Name webfood-to-queue-prod Event bus webfood-events-prod Rule type Rule with an event pattern Event pattern (JSON) see below { "detail-type": ["OrderCreated", "OrderStatusChanged", "PaymentSucceeded"] } → Target: SQS queue webfood-order-events-prod → Create rule.
Rule 2 — webfood-to-ws-prod → Lambda WsNotify Field Value Name webfood-to-ws-prod Event bus webfood-events-prod Event pattern (JSON) see below { "detail-type": ["OrderCreated", "OrderStatusChanged", "PaymentSucceeded", "VoucherPublished"] } → Target: Lambda function webfood-ws-notify → Create rule.
Console adds EventBridge invoke permission automatically. If missing: Lambda webfood-ws-notify → Permissions → principal events.amazonaws.com.
Next: 5.6.2 REST API.

title: IAM Role
content:
IAM Role for Lambda Console: IAM → Roles → Create role webfood-lambda-role-prod
Attach AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole, AWSXRayDaemonWriteAccess and inline policy (Secrets Manager, S3, EventBridge, DynamoDB, SES, SQS, WebSocket).
Next: 5.5.2 Lambda Functions.

title: Overview
content:
Live deployed demo Reviewers can open the running WebFood system at:
https://dh9xhc3jrccu4.cloudfront.net/
This is the CloudFront distribution for the project (frontend + API) after completing the workshop steps.
Workshop Objectives After completing this workshop, you will:
Deploy the full WebFood platform on AWS Serverless. Understand the request flow from CloudFront → API Gateway → Lambda → MongoDB Atlas. Configure event-driven pipeline: EventBridge → SQS/Worker/SES and WebSocket notify. Operate React frontend on S3 + CloudFront with WAF protection. Architecture Overview WebFood uses a serverless model: no EC2, all compute runs on Lambda. Frontend is a React SPA built statically and hosted on S3, distributed via CloudFront.
Main Processing Flows Order (REST): Browser → CloudFront /api/* → API Gateway REST → Lambda webfood-api → MongoDB → publish EventBridge. Email (async): EventBridge rule → SQS → Lambda webfood-worker → SES sends confirmation email. Real-time (WebSocket): EventBridge rule → Lambda webfood-ws-notify → API Gateway WebSocket → client receives voucher/order toast. WS Connection: Client opens WebSocket → Lambda webfood-ws-connect → saves connectionId to DynamoDB. Lambda Functions Function Handler Role webfood-api src/lambda.handler REST API (Express app) webfood-worker src/lambda-worker.handler Process SQS, send SES email webfood-ws-connect src/lambda-ws-connect.handler Route $connect webfood-ws-disconnect src/lambda-ws-disconnect.handler Route $disconnect webfood-ws-notify src/lambda-ws-notify.handler Push message via WebSocket EventBridge Event Types OrderCreated — new order OrderStatusChanged — status update PaymentSucceeded — successful MoMo payment VoucherPublished — admin publishes voucher (WebSocket only) Workshop Roadmap Section Contents 5.2 Prerequisites Accounts, tools 5.3 Secrets & Storage Secrets Manager, S3 5.4 Data & Messaging DynamoDB, SQS, EventBridge, SNS 5.5 Lambda IAM, functions, SQS trigger 5.6 API & Events Rules, REST, WebSocket 5.7 CDN & Operations WAF, CloudFront, SES, frontend 5.8 Smoke Test Smoke test 5.9 Teardown Delete resources

title: Secrets Manager
content:

Secret type: Other type of secret Plaintext tab, paste application JSON (MONGO_URI, JWT, MoMo, SES…) Secret name: webfood/prod/app-secrets → Store 📌 Note the Secret ARN Next: 5.3.2 S3 Buckets.

title: Worklog
content:
This worklog documents the 12-week internship (20/04/2026 – 10/07/2026) in the First Cloud AI Journey program. Early weeks focus on learning AWS fundamentals; from week 8 onward, the team project WebFood — an online food ordering platform on AWS Serverless architecture.
Week 1: Getting started with AWS, EC2, and AWS CLI
Week 2: Amazon S3, RDS, and deploying website on EC2
Week 3: VPC, IAM, CloudWatch, and Auto Scaling
Week 4: Amazon Lightsail and EC2 Auto Scaling
Week 5: CloudWatch, SNS, and Route 53 hybrid DNS
Week 6: AWS CLI on EC2 Ubuntu and Windows Server
Week 7: Web deployment on EC2 and Application Load Balancer
Week 8: Designing WebFood project architecture
Week 9: Local WebFood development and AWS infrastructure deploy
Week 10: Deploying Lambda, API Gateway, CloudFront
Week 11: Full system testing and documentation
Week 12: Project summary and FCAJ report completion

title: Worklog Week 1
content: Week 1 Objectives: Get familiar with the internship environment and FCAJ program members. Learn workplace rules, workflows, and basic requirements. Build foundational cloud computing and AWS service knowledge. Get familiar with AWS Management Console and AWS CLI. Practice creating AWS account, installing AWS CLI, and deploying a basic EC2 instance. Tasks for this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference 1 Get familiar with FCAJ; learn workplace rules and workflows. 20/04/2026 20/04/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ 2 Learn AWS and service groups: Compute, Storage, Networking, Database. 21/04/2026 21/04/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ 3 Create AWS Free Tier; learn Console; install and configure AWS CLI. 22/04/2026 22/04/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ 4 Learn EC2: Instance Type, AMI, EBS, Elastic IP, SSH. 23/04/2026 23/04/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ 5 Practice creating EC2 instance and SSH connection. 24/04/2026 24/04/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 1 Achievements: Got familiar with internship environment and FCAJ members. Understood workplace rules and workflows. Understood AWS overview and main service groups. Created AWS Free Tier and configured AWS CLI successfully. Practiced EC2 and SSH connection. Combined Console and CLI for AWS resource management.

title: FCAJ AI Meetup (06/06/2026)
content:
Meetup 06/06/2026 Report — First Cloud Journey AI Event purpose Share practical knowledge from the First Cloud Journey AI community Cover Cloud, DevOps, security, multiplayer gaming, and soft skills Learn career pathways and effective teamwork practices Event details Event name: First Cloud Journey AI Meetup (06/06/2026) Date: June 6, 2026 Location: 26th Floor — AWS Vietnam Office (Bitexco Tower, 2 Hai Trieu Street, Saigon Ward, Ho Chi Minh City) Role: Attendee Slide source: Meetup Google Drive folder Speakers & opening slides Below is the first slide from each speaker’s deck.
1. Bảo Huỳnh — Docker: A containerization technology Junior Cloud Native Developer (Endava Vietnam), Founder / Head Lab (ITea Lab) Introduces Docker as a foundation for Cloud Native and DevOps workflows 2. Lê Hoàng Gia Đại — WAF + ML for Cyber Attack Detection AWS G3 — Machine Learning-based Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS) on AWS Combines AWS WAF with Machine Learning for cyber attack detection 3. Nguyễn Quốc Bảo — Multiplayer in the Cloud Connects Godot clients to AWS WebSockets (Lambda, DynamoDB) Demonstrates a realtime multiplayer architecture on AWS 4. Trương Phước — The Art of Effective Teamwork Soft skills session on effective teamwork (“Many hands make light work”) Balances the technical talks with collaboration practices 5. Việt Phát — GraphRAG with Amazon Bedrock & Amazon Neptune Builds a graph knowledge base / GraphRAG on Amazon Neptune and Bedrock Explores graph-based RAG beyond pure vector retrieval 6. Vinh Trần — From IT Helpdesk to Senior Sysadmin System Administrator at Central Retail Group Shares a self-learning journey and a path toward Cloud / DevOps Highlights Containers & Cloud Native: Docker packages apps consistently across local and cloud environments. Security on AWS: WAF + ML/NIDS goes beyond static rules for attack detection. Realtime on AWS: WebSocket + Lambda + DynamoDB fits multiplayer / realtime updates (relevant to WebFood WebSocket). GraphRAG: Neptune + Bedrock for graph knowledge, complementary to vector RAG. Career & soft skills: Helpdesk → Sysadmin → Cloud/DevOps; teamwork alongside technical depth. Takeaways & application Better understanding of containers compared with the serverless model used in WebFood. Connecting WebFood’s WAF layer with ML-based detection ideas. Reinforcing API Gateway WebSocket knowledge via the Godot multiplayer case. Extra context on GraphRAG / Neptune for AWS AI experiments and blogs. Practical framing for career storytelling and teamwork during the internship. Experience The meetup felt community-driven: deep technical talks (WAF+ML, GraphRAG, WebSocket) alongside soft skills and career stories. Reviewing each speaker’s opening slide made it easy to grasp the “hook” of every session.
Overall, Meetup 06/06 broadened my Cloud/AI perspective beyond the WebFood serverless lab and connected me with the First Cloud Journey AI community.

title: Prerequisites
content:
Before deploying WebFood, prepare the following accounts, tools, and information.
1. AWS Account & Region Sign in to AWS Management Console. Select region us-east-1 (N. Virginia) — required for CloudFront WAF. Note your <ACCOUNT_ID> (12 digits) from the account menu. 2. Local Environment Requirement Details Node.js Version 20+ Git Clone WebFood repository PowerShell / Terminal npm install, Compress-Archive 3. MongoDB Atlas MongoDB Atlas cluster (free tier works), database webappfood. Connection string mongodb+srv://user:pass@cluster.xxx.mongodb.net/webappfood. Network Access: add 0.0.0.0/0. 4. MoMo Sandbox partnerCode, accessKey, secretKey Endpoint: https://test-payment.momo.vn/v2/gateway/api/create 5. Amazon SES 1 sender email (FROM_EMAIL) — verify in SES. 1 email for CloudWatch alerts via SNS. 6. IAM Permissions Permissions to create/manage: Lambda, API Gateway, S3, CloudFront, WAF, DynamoDB, SQS, EventBridge, SNS, Secrets Manager, IAM Role, CloudWatch.
Checklist Region = us-east-1 MongoDB Atlas connection string ready MoMo sandbox credentials SES email verified Node.js 20+ installed WebFood source code (backend/, frontend/) on local machine Next: 5.3 Secrets & Storage.

title: Proposal
content:
In this section, I present the workshop proposal for WebFood — an online food ordering platform deployed on AWS Serverless architecture.
Live demo: https://dh9xhc3jrccu4.cloudfront.net/
WebFood — Online Food Ordering Platform AWS Serverless Solution for F&B E-Commerce 1. Executive Summary WebFood is an online food ordering platform supporting three user roles: Customer (browse menu, cart, MoMo payment), Merchant (manage products, orders, statistics), and Admin (approve restaurants, vouchers, platform finances). The application is built with React (frontend) and Node.js/Express (backend), with business data stored on MongoDB Atlas.
Within the workshop scope, the project is deployed on AWS using a full serverless architecture: CloudFront + WAF for frontend and media delivery, API Gateway REST + Lambda for APIs, API Gateway WebSocket + DynamoDB for real-time notifications, and EventBridge + SQS + Lambda Worker for asynchronous processing (SES email). The solution is suitable for demo/learning environments with low operating costs and request-based scalability.
2. Problem Statement Current Problem
Traditional food ordering often relies on phone calls, manual chat, or fragmented systems between customers and restaurants. As the number of restaurants and orders grows, tracking order status, sending notifications, and managing vouchers becomes difficult to control. Deploying on fixed servers (EC2) also requires maintenance costs even without traffic.
Solution
WebFood centralizes the ordering flow on a multi-role web platform, combined with MoMo sandbox payment and an event-driven architecture on AWS. When business events occur (order created, status changed, payment succeeded, voucher published), the system publishes events to EventBridge while sending email via SQS/Worker/SES and pushing real-time notifications via WebSocket.
Benefits and ROI
Reduced infrastructure costs through pay-per-use model (Lambda, API Gateway, S3). Separation of static frontend (S3/CloudFront) and API backend (Lambda), easier to maintain and scale. Event-driven design enables feature extension (email, notifications) without modifying core API. Estimated cost of approximately $8–15/month for demo workload (see section 6), significantly lower than maintaining EC2 24/7. 3. Solution Architecture WebFood applies serverless architecture per docs/wedfood.drawio, deployed in us-east-1 region (required for WAF on CloudFront).
AWS Services Used
Group Service Role Edge CloudFront, WAF, CloudFront Functions CDN, protection, SPA routing Storage S3 (frontend + media) Host React build, store product images Compute Lambda (5 functions) Express API, email worker, WebSocket handlers API API Gateway REST + WebSocket REST /api/*, real-time $connect/$disconnect Database DynamoDB Store WebSocket connectionId by userId Messaging EventBridge, SQS + DLQ, SNS Event bus, worker queue, alerts Security Secrets Manager, IAM, OAC JWT/MoMo/MongoDB credentials, bucket policy Observability CloudWatch, X-Ray Logs, alarms, distributed tracing Email SES Order confirmation, password reset Component Design
Frontend: React 19 + Vite, static build uploaded to S3, served via CloudFront. ApiFunction (webfood-api): Wraps Express app via @codegenie/serverless-express, connects to MongoDB Atlas, publishes events to EventBridge. WorkerFunction (webfood-worker): SQS trigger, sends email via SES on OrderCreated, OrderStatusChanged, PaymentSucceeded. WebSocket Lambdas: ws-connect/ws-disconnect manage DynamoDB; ws-notify receives EventBridge events, pushes messages to clients. MongoDB Atlas: Primary database (User, Restaurant, Product, Order, Voucher, etc.) — outside AWS VPC. 4. Technical Implementation Implementation Phases
Local development (Weeks 4–6): React frontend + Express/Mongoose backend, JWT auth, ordering flow, MoMo sandbox integration. AWS architecture design (Weeks 7–8): Draw wedfood.drawio, write SAM template (infra/template.yaml), standardize Lambda handlers. AWS deployment (Weeks 9–10): Manual Console deployment per DEPLOY_CONSOLE.md (or sam deploy) — Secrets Manager, S3, Lambda, API Gateway, CloudFront. Testing & optimization (Weeks 11–12): End-to-end smoke test, enable X-Ray, CloudWatch alarms, update CloudFront/MoMo environment variables. Technical Requirements
Local: Node.js 20+, MongoDB, npm for backend/ and frontend/. AWS: Account with permissions to create Lambda, API Gateway, S3, CloudFront, WAF, EventBridge, DynamoDB, SQS, SES. Third-party: MongoDB Atlas cluster, MoMo Test account, SES-verified email. IaC: AWS SAM (template.yaml) or Console click-ops for workshop reporting purposes. 5. Timeline & Milestones Phase Timeline Key Milestones Learn AWS basics Weeks 1–3 (17/04 – 08/05/2026) Free Tier, EC2, S3, VPC, IAM, CLI Develop WebFood locally Weeks 4–8 (11/05 – 12/06/2026) Learn Lightsail, ELB, CloudWatch; design WebFood Design serverless Weeks 8–9 (08/06 – 19/06/2026) wedfood.drawio, SAM template, Lambda adapter Deploy production Weeks 9–10 (15/06 – 26/06/2026) Console deploy, CloudFront live Testing & reporting Weeks 11–14 (29/06 – 30/07/2026) Smoke test, workshop doc, Hugo site 6. Budget Estimation Estimated monthly cost for demo workload (~10,000 API requests, ~5 GB CloudFront transfer):
Service Estimated Cost/Month AWS Lambda (5 functions) ~$1.50 API Gateway REST + WebSocket ~$2.00 Amazon CloudFront ~$2.50 Amazon S3 (frontend + media) ~$0.50 DynamoDB (on-demand) ~$0.25 EventBridge + SQS ~$0.10 AWS WAF ~$5.00 Amazon SES ~$0.10 Secrets Manager ~$0.40 Total ~$12.35/month (~$148/year) View details on AWS Pricing Calculator.
Or download budget estimation file.
7. Risk Assessment Risk Matrix
Risk Impact Probability Lambda cold start Medium Medium MongoDB Atlas connection loss High Low MoMo sandbox unresponsive Medium Low WAF/CloudFront cost exceeds Free Tier Medium Medium Lambda zip > 50 MB (node_modules) Medium Medium Mitigation Strategies
Provisioned concurrency for webfood-api if needed (production). MongoDB Atlas: IP whitelist, connection pool monitoring. CloudWatch billing alarm + SNS notification. npm install --omit=dev when packaging Lambda; upload via S3 if zip is large. Contingency Plan
Fallback to local mode (Express + MongoDB) when AWS has issues during demo. SAM template (infra/template.yaml) for quick stack recreation after teardown. 8. Expected Outcomes Technical Improvements
End-to-end ordering flow: customer places order → API Lambda → EventBridge → SES email + WebSocket voucher toast. Centralized administration: admin approves restaurants, merchant updates menu, customer tracks orders in real-time. Scalable architecture: add new event types on EventBridge without API refactoring. Long-term Value
Reference platform for serverless e-commerce projects on AWS. Comprehensive hands-on experience: IaC (SAM), event-driven, CDN, WebSocket, observability. Can extend to production with Cognito auth, CI/CD pipeline, and multi-region.

title: Blogs Posted
content:
This section lists the blog posts I published in the AWS Study Group VN group during the First Cloud AI Journey program.
Blog 1 — AI Agents on Amazon Bedrock & AgentCore A post sharing perspectives on AI Agent architecture on AWS: AI is not just a chatbot — it can understand goals, plan, use tools, and perform real work through Amazon Bedrock / AgentCore. Key themes include multi-agent design, tool use, and AI as a “worker” inside the system.

title: EventBridge & SNS
content:
EventBridge Custom Event Bus Console: EventBridge → Event buses → Create event bus
Field Value Name webfood-events-prod Click Create. 📌 Note the bus ARN (arn:aws:events:us-east-1:<ACCOUNT_ID>:event-bus/webfood-events-prod).
Do not create Rules yet — Lambda webfood-ws-notify does not exist. Configure rules in 5.6.1 EventBridge Rules.
SNS Alert Topic Console: SNS → Topics → Create topic
Field Value Type Standard Name webfood-alarms-prod Click Create topic. 📌 Note the ARN.
Subscriptions tab → Create subscription:
Field Value Protocol Email Endpoint your CloudWatch alert email Click Create subscription → open email → Confirm subscription (required to receive alerts).
Next: 5.5 Lambda.

title: Observability & SES
content:
Update Secrets Manager After CloudFront domain is ready, update secret webfood/prod/app-secrets:
MOMO_REDIRECT_URL = https://<cloudfront-domain>/payment-result MOMO_IPN_URL = https://<cloudfront-domain>/api/payment/momo/ipn FRONTEND_URL = https://<cloudfront-domain> Lambda webfood-api → Environment variables → MEDIA_CDN_URL = https://<cloudfront-domain>.
Force new cold start: re-save Lambda environment variables so cached secrets refresh.
CloudWatch Alarms Console: CloudWatch → Alarms → Create alarm (3 times). Each alarm notifies SNS topic webfood-alarms-prod from 5.4.3.
Alarm 1 — webfood-api errors Metric: Lambda → webfood-api → Errors, Statistic Sum, Period 5 minutes Condition: Greater/Equal 1 Missing data: Treat missing data as good Notification: SNS webfood-alarms-prod Name: webfood-api-errors-prod Alarm 2 — webfood-worker errors Same as Alarm 1, change Function → webfood-worker, alarm name → webfood-worker-errors-prod.
Alarm 3 — DLQ messages Metric: SQS → webfood-order-events-dlq-prod → ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible Statistic: Maximum, Period 5 minutes Condition: Greater/Equal 1 Notification: SNS webfood-alarms-prod Name: webfood-dlq-messages-prod Verify SES SES → Verified identities → verify FROM_EMAIL and test recipient emails (SES Sandbox only sends to verified addresses).
Next: 5.7.4 Frontend Deploy.

title: Secrets & Storage
content:
Create Secrets Manager (app configuration) and S3 buckets (frontend + media). These resources are independent of Lambda — create them first.
Sub-sections 5.3.1 Secrets Manager 5.3.2 S3 Buckets 📌 Note the secret ARN and bucket names — later steps (IAM, Lambda, CloudFront) will need them.

title: SQS Trigger
content:
SQS Trigger for Worker webfood-worker → Add trigger → SQS webfood-order-events-prod, batch size 5, enable Report batch item failures.
Next: 5.6 API & Events.

title: Worklog Week 3
content: Week 3 Objectives: Understand VPC and network design on AWS. Learn IAM. Learn CloudWatch. Understand Auto Scaling and Load Balancer. Tasks for this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference 1 Learn VPC: Subnet, Internet Gateway, NAT Gateway. 04/05/2026 04/05/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ 2 Security Group and NACL; open SSH/HTTP ports. 05/05/2026 05/05/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ 3 IAM: User, Group, Role, Policy. Create user and IAM Role for EC2. 06/05/2026 06/05/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ 4 CloudWatch: Metrics, Logs, Alarms; monitor EC2 CPU. 07/05/2026 07/05/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ 5 Auto Scaling Group and Load Balancer; test scale up/down. 08/05/2026 08/05/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 3 Achievements: Understood VPC, EC2, RDS, S3 architecture. Learned network security and IAM. Monitored system with CloudWatch. Can design basic web systems on AWS.

title: Data & Messaging
content:
Create data storage and async messaging services: DynamoDB (WebSocket connections), SQS (order queue), EventBridge (event bus), SNS (alerts).
Sub-sections 5.4.1 DynamoDB 5.4.2 SQS 5.4.3 EventBridge & SNS

title: Events Participated
content:
During my internship in the First Cloud AI Journey (FCAJ) program at AWS Vietnam, I attended two community events at the AWS office (26th Floor, Bitexco Tower). Each session expanded my Cloud/AI knowledge, soft skills, and connection with the learner community.
Event 1 — AWS Community Day (30/05/2026) Event name: AWS Community Day — First Cloud Journey AI
Date: May 30, 2026
Location: 26th Floor — AWS Vietnam Office (Bitexco Tower, 2 Hai Trieu Street, Saigon Ward, Ho Chi Minh City)
Role: Attendee
Highlights: Sessions on AWS Cloud Quest & Floci, hackathon demo SynthHunter, soft skills (confidence, procrastination), a community product (Tử vi Đại Việt), and DevOps thinking (DevOps Before Disaster).
Takeaways: Stronger AWS learning path and cost awareness on a real account, clearer ways to present AI/hackathon projects, and soft skills that support the internship and WebFood deployment.
→ Read the full Event 1 report
Event 2 — First Cloud Journey AI Meetup (06/06/2026) Event name: First Cloud Journey AI Meetup
Date: June 6, 2026
Location: 26th Floor — AWS Vietnam Office (Bitexco Tower, 2 Hai Trieu Street, Saigon Ward, Ho Chi Minh City)
Role: Attendee
Highlights: Six talks covering Docker/containerization, AWS WAF + Machine Learning (NIDS), realtime multiplayer with Godot + AWS WebSockets, GraphRAG on Amazon Neptune & Bedrock, effective teamwork, and a career path from Helpdesk → Sysadmin → Cloud/DevOps.
Takeaways: Direct links to the WebFood architecture (WAF, WebSocket, serverless), plus broader context on containers, security, GraphRAG, and Cloud career planning.
→ Read the full Event 2 report

title: Frontend Deploy
content:
Build & Upload Frontend Create .env in frontend/:
VITE_API_URL=https://<cloudfront-domain>/api VITE_WS_URL=wss://<ws-api-id>.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/prod cd E:\workshop\WebFood\frontend npm run build aws s3 sync dist/ s3://webfood-frontend-<ACCOUNT_ID>-prod/ --delete Next: 5.8 Smoke Test.

title: Worklog Week 4
content: Week 4 Objectives: Learn Amazon Lightsail and cost optimization. Deploy simple application with Lightsail. Understand EC2 Auto Scaling and Launch Template. Practice Auto Scaling Group. Tasks for this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference 1 Learn Lightsail; compare Lightsail and EC2. 11/05/2026 11/05/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 2 Create Lightsail Instance; configure region, blueprint, plan. 12/05/2026 12/05/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 3 Lightsail networking; configure Static IP; SSH access. 13/05/2026 13/05/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 4 Learn EC2 Auto Scaling, Launch Template, Scaling Policy. 14/05/2026 14/05/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 5 Create Launch Template and Auto Scaling Group; test scaling. 15/05/2026 15/05/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 4 Achievements: Understood Lightsail for small apps with fixed cost. Created and managed Lightsail Instance, Static IP. Distinguished Lightsail vs EC2 Auto Scaling. Practiced Launch Template and Auto Scaling Group.

title: Lambda
content:
Create IAM Role, package code, deploy 5 Lambda functions, and attach SQS trigger to Worker.
Sub-sections 5.5.1 IAM Role 5.5.2 Lambda Functions 5.5.3 SQS Trigger

title: Worklog Week 5
content: Week 5 Objectives: Build system monitoring with CloudWatch. Set up CloudWatch Alarm and SNS. Learn Amazon Route 53. Practice hybrid DNS between on-premises and VPC. Tasks for this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference 1 CloudWatch overview: Metrics, Logs, Alarms, Dashboards. 18/05/2026 18/05/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 2 Monitor EC2: CPU, Network, Disk Activity. 19/05/2026 19/05/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 3 Create CloudWatch Alarm; integrate SNS notifications. 20/05/2026 20/05/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 4 Route 53 overview: Hosted Zone, Record, Resolver. 21/05/2026 21/05/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 5 Set up hybrid DNS with Route 53 Resolver Endpoint. 22/05/2026 22/05/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 5 Achievements: Monitored EC2 with CloudWatch Metrics and Dashboards. Created Alarms and integrated SNS. Understood Route 53 and hybrid DNS model. Learned monitoring and hybrid DNS benefits on AWS.

title: Workshop
content:
Deploy WebFood on AWS Serverless This workshop guides you through deploying the complete WebFood architecture on AWS Management Console (click-ops) with screenshots for each step. Sections are grouped by function for clarity.
Live demo (for reviewers): https://dh9xhc3jrccu4.cloudfront.net/
Reviewers can open this URL to explore the WebFood system deployed on AWS.
Required region: us-east-1 (WAF for CloudFront must be created in us-east-1).
Table of Contents # Section Contents 5.1 Overview Goals, architecture, main flows 5.2 Prerequisites Accounts, tools, checklist 5.3 Secrets & Storage Secrets Manager, S3 5.4 Data & Messaging DynamoDB, SQS, EventBridge, SNS 5.5 Lambda IAM, package code, functions, trigger 5.6 API & Events EventBridge rules, REST API, WebSocket 5.7 CDN & Operations WAF, CloudFront, CloudWatch, SES, frontend 5.8 Smoke Test Verify main application flows 5.9 Teardown Delete resources to avoid ongoing costs

title: API & Events
content:
Configure EventBridge rules (event routing) and create API Gateway REST + WebSocket.
Sub-sections 5.6.1 EventBridge Rules 5.6.2 REST API 5.6.3 WebSocket API

title: Self-Assessment
content:
During my internship at Amazon Web Services Viet Nam Company Limited from 17/04/2026 to 30/07/2026, under the Workforce Bootcamp – First Cloud AI Journey program, I had the opportunity to learn AWS cloud fundamentals and apply them in a real project.
I designed and deployed WebFood — an online food-ordering platform on an AWS Serverless architecture (CloudFront, WAF, S3, API Gateway, Lambda, EventBridge, SQS, DynamoDB, Secrets Manager, SES, CloudWatch). Through this work I improved full-stack development skills (React, Node.js/Express), cloud architecture design, console-based (click-ops) deployment, workshop documentation, and technical reporting.
Regarding work ethic, I aimed to complete weekly worklog tasks on time, followed program processes, and proactively asked mentors when I faced difficulties configuring AWS services.
To reflect objectively on the internship, I evaluate myself against the criteria below:
No. Criteria Description Good Fair Average 1 Professional knowledge & skills Understanding of the field, applying knowledge in practice, proficiency with tools, work quality ✅ ☐ ☐ 2 Ability to learn Ability to absorb new knowledge and learn quickly ✅ ☐ ☐ 3 Proactiveness Taking initiative, seeking out tasks without waiting for instructions ✅ ☐ ☐ 4 Sense of responsibility Completing tasks on time and ensuring quality ✅ ☐ ☐ 5 Discipline Adhering to schedules, rules, and work processes ☐ ✅ ☐ 6 Progressive mindset Willingness to receive feedback and improve oneself ✅ ☐ ☐ 7 Communication Presenting ideas and reporting work clearly ☐ ✅ ☐ 8 Teamwork Working effectively with colleagues and participating in teams ☐ ✅ ☐ 9 Professional conduct Respecting colleagues, partners, and the work environment ✅ ☐ ☐ 10 Problem-solving skills Identifying problems, proposing solutions, and showing creativity ☐ ✅ ☐ 11 Contribution to project/team Work effectiveness, innovative ideas, recognition from the team ✅ ☐ ☐ 12 Overall General evaluation of the entire internship period ✅ ☐ ☐ Strengths Understood the end-to-end serverless flow of WebFood: from CloudFront/WAF to Lambda, EventBridge, SQS, and WebSocket. Manually deployed resources on the AWS Console in the correct dependency order (Secrets Manager, S3, DynamoDB, IAM, Lambda, API Gateway, CDN). Delivered Proposal, Workshop, and Worklog documentation tied to a real project instead of only following sample labs. Proactively learned additional services (EventBridge rules, CloudWatch Alarms, SNS) and added screenshots to the report. Needs Improvement Strengthen IaC skills (AWS SAM / CloudFormation) to reduce reliance on click-ops and make environments easier to recreate. Improve communication and presentation when explaining complex architecture (event-driven, WebSocket) to listeners less familiar with cloud. Build stronger operations mindset: monitor Free Tier cost, tear down resources regularly, and handle cold starts and DLQ failures more systematically. Practice CI/CD and automated testing for frontend/backend before deploying to AWS. Post-internship Direction After the FCAJ program, I plan to deepen my knowledge of AWS services (especially serverless and observability), evolve WebFood closer to production (Cognito, CI/CD pipeline, cost optimization), and build experience toward a Cloud / Backend Developer role.

title: Worklog Week 6
content: Week 6 Objectives: Use AWS CLI on EC2 (Ubuntu and Windows Server). Configure AWS CLI and practice basic commands. Understand IAM Role for EC2. Compare CLI on both operating systems. Tasks for this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference 1 AWS CLI overview; review EC2, IAM User, IAM Role. 25/05/2026 25/05/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 2 EC2 Ubuntu: SSH, install AWS CLI, check version. 26/05/2026 26/05/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 3 Configure AWS CLI on Ubuntu; sts, ec2, s3 commands. 27/05/2026 27/05/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 4 EC2 Windows Server: RDP, install and configure AWS CLI. 28/05/2026 28/05/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 5 Attach IAM Role to EC2; compare CLI on Ubuntu vs Windows. 29/05/2026 29/05/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 6 Achievements: Installed and configured AWS CLI on Ubuntu and Windows Server. Practiced CLI commands for EC2, S3, IAM management. Attached IAM Role to EC2 instead of using Access Key directly. Understood CLI benefits for AWS administration automation.

title: CDN & Operations
content:
Final deploy phase: WAF, CloudFront, update secrets, CloudWatch, SES, and upload frontend.
Sub-sections 5.7.1 WAF 5.7.2 CloudFront 5.7.3 Observability 5.7.4 Frontend Deploy

title: Sharing and Feedback
content:
Below are my personal reflections on participating in the First Cloud AI Journey (FCAJ) program at AWS Viet Nam, shared to help the program team improve future cohorts.
Overall Evaluation 1. Working Environment
The program provides an open learning environment that encourages self-learning and hands-on practice on AWS. Communication between interns and mentors is friendly, making it easy to ask questions when difficulties arise. I especially appreciate the structured learning path and the chance to work on a real project instead of only following sample labs.
2. Support from Mentor / Team Admin
Mentors explain clearly when I do not understand something, while still encouraging me to try and fix issues myself before giving the answer. Program materials and the Hugo report template gave me a clear structure for completing the Worklog, Proposal, and Workshop. The admin team also kept procedures and timelines stable throughout the internship.
3. Relevance of Work to Academic Major
As an Information Technology student, I already had a basic programming foundation. FCAJ helped me connect software knowledge with AWS cloud infrastructure — from compute, storage, and networking to security and monitoring. This complements my major and supports a career path related to cloud / backend roles.
4. Learning & Skill Development Opportunities
During the internship, I gained several new skills:
Working with common AWS services through the Console Designing and presenting cloud architecture for a real project Writing technical documentation (Proposal, Workshop, weekly Worklog) Managing Free Tier cost and tearing down resources after demos Sharing through Study Group blogs and events These are practical experiences that are hard to get from classroom theory alone.
5. Company Culture & Team Spirit
FCAJ’s learning culture is positive: people are willing to share experiences, blogs, and events. The “learn by doing” spirit fits the program’s goals and helps interns feel part of a community, even during a short internship period.
6. Internship Policies / Benefits
The program offers a structured AWS learning path (from fundamentals to a project), sample materials, and mentor support. Being able to practice on an AWS account and finish a product/report at the end of the internship is a major benefit for interns.
Additional Questions What did you find most satisfying during your internship?
I was most satisfied when I could deploy an architecture on AWS end-to-end and see the system actually run after the configuration steps. That moment made the value of what I learned in the program concrete.
What do you think the program should improve for future interns?
Add IaC guidance (SAM/CloudFormation) alongside click-ops so environments are easier to recreate. Provide a clearer cost / teardown checklist from the start to avoid unexpected charges. Offer more deep-dive sessions on observability and security (IAM, WAF, CloudWatch). If recommending to a friend, would you suggest they intern here? Why or why not?
Yes. FCAJ suits students who want hands-on AWS experience, mentor support, and a concrete product/report after the internship. I would recommend it to friends interested in cloud or backend roles.
Suggestions & Expectations Do you have any suggestions to improve the internship experience?
Add a short workshop on CI/CD after interns are familiar with manual deployment. Standardize deploy / teardown checklists so interns spend less time debugging configuration issues. Organize peer reviews across teams to learn from each other’s projects. Would you like to continue this program in the future?
Yes. I want to keep deepening my knowledge of AWS services and apply them to more real-world projects later.
Any other comments
Thank you to the FCAJ team and AWS Viet Nam for enabling hands-on learning. This internship made me more confident working with cloud and clarified my career direction after graduation.

title: Worklog Week 7
content: Week 7 Objectives: Deploy web application on EC2. Learn Elastic Load Balancing. Configure ALB, Target Group, Health Check. Combine EC2 and ALB for high availability. Tasks for this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference 1 Web deployment on EC2; review Security Group, SSH. 01/06/2026 01/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 2 EC2 Ubuntu: install Apache/Nginx, deploy simple website. 02/06/2026 02/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 3 ELB overview: ALB, NLB; Target Group, Listener. 03/06/2026 03/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 4 Create second EC2; Target Group; Health Check. 04/06/2026 04/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 5 Create ALB; test load balancing via Load Balancer DNS. 05/06/2026 05/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 7 Achievements: Deployed website on EC2 Ubuntu. Understood ALB, Target Group, Health Check. Configured ALB distributing traffic to 2 EC2 instances. Learned HA benefits combining EC2 and Load Balancer.

title: Smoke Test
content:
Smoke Test Access CloudFront URL and verify main flows:
1. Frontend Open https://<cloudfront-domain>/ → WebFood homepage displays. Register / login as customer. 2. REST API Browse menu, add items to cart. Place order → verify order appears in history. 3. MoMo Payment (sandbox) Select MoMo payment → sandbox redirect → return to app. 4. Event-driven After placing order: check SES email (if configured). Admin publishes voucher → client receives toast via WebSocket. 5. Merchant / Admin Login as merchant → manage products, update order status. Login as admin → approve restaurants, manage vouchers. Next: 5.9 Teardown.

title: Worklog Week 8
content: Week 8 Objectives: Define WebFood project — online food ordering platform. Analyze requirements and design system architecture. Draw wedfood.drawio and select AWS Serverless services. Present architecture to FCAJ mentors and receive feedback. Tasks for this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference 1 Team meeting: discuss WebFood idea; assign tasks. 08/06/2026 08/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 2 Analyze requirements: Customer, Merchant, Admin; choose React + Node.js stack. 09/06/2026 09/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 3 Design AWS Serverless architecture; draw wedfood.drawio. 10/06/2026 10/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 4 Present architecture to FCAJ mentors; receive feedback. 11/06/2026 11/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 5 Finalize architecture; plan WebFood deployment. 12/06/2026 12/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 8 Achievements: Defined WebFood project with 3 user roles. Designed serverless architecture: CloudFront, Lambda, API Gateway, EventBridge, SQS, WebSocket. Completed wedfood.drawio diagram. Received mentor feedback and adjusted architecture. Created deployment plan for upcoming weeks.

title: Teardown
content:
Teardown — Resource Cleanup When no longer needed (avoid ongoing costs), delete in reverse deploy order:
CloudFront Distribution (disable → delete, wait for deployed) WAF WebACL API Gateway REST + WebSocket Lambda functions (5) EventBridge rules + event bus SQS queues + DLQ DynamoDB table SNS topic + subscriptions S3 buckets (must be empty first) Secrets Manager secret IAM Role webfood-lambda-role-prod CloudWatch Alarms Note: MongoDB Atlas cluster is outside AWS — delete separately on Atlas if not needed.
Workshop Conclusion After this workshop, you have successfully deployed the WebFood platform with a complete AWS Serverless architecture: CDN, API, real-time WebSocket, event-driven email, and observability.

title: Worklog Week 9
content: Week 9 Objectives: Develop WebFood locally: React frontend + Express/MongoDB backend. Write SAM template (infra/template.yaml). Start AWS infrastructure deploy: Secrets Manager, S3, DynamoDB, SQS, EventBridge. Integrate JWT auth and basic ordering flow. Tasks for this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference 1 Review architecture; setup MongoDB Atlas; initialize WebFood repo. 15/06/2026 15/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 2 Dev backend: User/Restaurant/Product/Order models; auth API. 16/06/2026 16/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 3 Dev frontend: Home, Menu, Cart, Checkout pages. 17/06/2026 17/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 4 Write template.yaml; deploy Secrets Manager, S3, DynamoDB, SQS, EventBridge. 18/06/2026 18/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 5 Package Lambda zip; create IAM Role; test API locally. 19/06/2026 19/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 9 Achievements: Developed WebFood locally with React + Express + MongoDB. Completed JWT auth and basic ordering flow. Wrote SAM template and deployed foundation infrastructure on AWS Console. Prepared Lambda package and IAM Role for next phase.

title: Worklog Week 10
content: Week 10 Objectives: Deploy 5 Lambda functions and API Gateway REST + WebSocket. Configure EventBridge rules, SQS trigger, WAF, CloudFront. Integrate MoMo sandbox and SES email. Test end-to-end ordering flow. Tasks for this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference 1 Create 5 Lambdas: api, worker, ws-connect/disconnect/notify. 22/06/2026 22/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 2 API Gateway REST + WebSocket; EventBridge rules. 23/06/2026 23/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 3 WAF WebACL; CloudFront Distribution; OAC bucket policy. 24/06/2026 24/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 4 Build & upload frontend; update CloudFront/MoMo secret URLs. 25/06/2026 25/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 5 Test ordering, MoMo payment, SES email, WebSocket notify. 26/06/2026 26/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 10 Achievements: Fully deployed Lambda, API Gateway REST/WebSocket on AWS. Configured EventBridge → SQS/Worker/SES and WebSocket notify. Completed CloudFront + WAF + frontend deployment. Tested ordering flow and fixed issues found. Presented progress to FCAJ mentors and received optimization feedback.

title: Worklog Week 11
content: Week 11 Objectives: Full system testing of WebFood on AWS. Verify CloudFront, API Gateway, Lambda, EventBridge, SQS, SES, WebSocket. Enable CloudWatch Alarms, X-Ray tracing. Complete DEPLOY_CONSOLE.md and workshop report documentation. Tasks for this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference 1 Smoke test frontend via CloudFront; verify API /api/*. 29/06/2026 29/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 2 Test EventBridge → SQS Worker → SES; WebSocket voucher notify. 30/06/2026 30/06/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 3 Verify MongoDB Atlas, DynamoDB connections, Secrets Manager. 01/07/2026 01/07/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 4 CloudWatch Alarms + SNS; X-Ray traces; complete DEPLOY_CONSOLE.md. 02/07/2026 02/07/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 5 Team project review; prepare Proposal and Workshop for FCAJ report. 03/07/2026 03/07/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 11 Achievements: Successfully tested CloudFront → API Gateway → Lambda → MongoDB flow. Confirmed event-driven pipeline: EventBridge, SQS, SES, WebSocket stable. Verified WAF, Secrets Manager, DynamoDB connections. Enabled CloudWatch/X-Ray and completed deployment documentation. Prepared Proposal + Workshop content for Hugo report.

title: Worklog Week 12
content: Week 12 Objectives: Review entire WebFood project after deployment. Complete technical documentation and architecture diagrams. Summarize results and evaluate system effectiveness. Complete FCAJ internship report (Hugo site). Tasks for this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference 1 System review; verify Customer/Merchant/Admin features. 06/07/2026 06/07/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 2 Verify processing flow between frontend, API, database, events. 07/07/2026 07/07/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 3 Complete docs: wedfood.drawio, SAM template, README. 08/07/2026 08/07/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 4 Complete Hugo report: Worklog, Proposal, Workshop. 09/07/2026 09/07/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ 5 Summarize project results; evaluate and reflect on lessons learned. 10/07/2026 10/07/2026 https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 12 Achievements: Confirmed WebFood runs stably on AWS Serverless. Completed technical documentation and deployment guide. Evaluated effectiveness of CloudFront, Lambda, API Gateway, EventBridge, SQS, SES, DynamoDB, WAF. Completed FCAJ report with Worklog, Proposal, Workshop. Learned lessons about serverless design, event-driven architecture, and teamwork.
